executive_summary.opening_paragraph: This document is the output of our discovery process. Over the past 60 days we have gathered financial, legal, tax, and operational data on you and your business, met with you both, reviewed three years of returns and statements, and analyzed the operating agreement, ownership structure, and current insurance program for Holloway Industrial Solutions. What follows is our recommended path forward — both immediate priorities and the longer-term strategy that protects what you have built and accelerates what you are building.
executive_summary.two_themes_paragraph: Two themes shape this plan. First, HIS is approaching a value range ($32M – $48M preliminary) where the cost of inaction on entity, estate, and tax planning compounds rapidly — every quarter the business grows without a holdco, a recap, or a real estate split is a quarter of foregone planning leverage. Second, you have unsolicited inbound interest from both strategic and financial buyers, which means a 3–5 year transaction window is realistic and possibly shorter; the planning we do now determines how much of the value you keep.
executive_summary.what_this_means_closer: Acted on together, the five priorities above produce roughly $7M – $13M of estate-tax savings on the trust and gifting layers alone, plus restructured liability protection sized to the actual balance sheet — and they unlock the larger downstream architecture (GRAT, IDGT sale, SLAT, dynasty/GST allocation, QSBS multiplication) that compounds into the eight- and nine-figure range across the planning horizon. The combined impact funds professional fees many times over, and — more importantly — it positions HIS, the household, and the children's trusts for a transaction in 3–5 years from a far stronger structural starting point than where you sit today.

our_process.intro_paragraph: Financial planning is not a one-time event, and this document is not the finish line. It is the structured output of a 60-day discovery process and the input to a multi-year working relationship. We have organized our work into four stages so you always know where we are in the process and what comes next.

stages.name: Discovery
stages.body: Completed. Over the past 60 days we gathered three years of business and personal returns, the HIS operating agreement, the 2014 wills, the personal balance sheet, and the existing insurance program. We met with both of you, walked through goals, ranked priorities, and stress-tested assumptions. The quality of what follows depends entirely on the quality of that input — and you gave us a complete picture.

stages.name: Plan delivery
stages.body: Today. This document. We will walk it together, you will mark anything you want to discuss or push back on, and we will agree on the top three priorities to execute against first. We have already lined up introductory calls with the estate attorney, M&A counsel, and CPA candidates we are recommending, so the partner bench is ready when you decide to move.

stages.name: Implementation
stages.body: Beginning at our delivery meeting. PSA quarterbacks. Each recommendation in the sections that follow names the specific partner who executes (Estate Attorney, M&A Counsel, CPA, Insurance Broker, Banker, Valuation Provider, Specialty Tax Credits firm). We coordinate the sequencing, route documents, hold deadlines, and surface decisions that need your input. The Implementation Roadmap at the back of this document is the project plan.

stages.name: Ongoing review
stages.body: Continuous. Tax law changes, the business grows, the children get older, the §7520 rate moves, a buyer makes an approach. The plan is a living document — we re-validate it on the cadence laid out in the Meeting Cadence section, and we trigger ad-hoc reviews whenever a material event happens.

our_process.how_to_read_paragraph: The Executive Summary is your 90-second readout. The Findings & Observations section explains where you stand today — what is working and where the gaps are. The Recommendations sections are our specific guidance, with Business first (Sections 1–7) and Personal second (Sections 8–12); each recommendation opens with the strategic frame, then names the actions, partners, dollar exposure, and timing. The Implementation Roadmap is your project plan organized by time bucket. The Decisions Needed page is the short list of questions we need answered to move forward. The Glossary defines every technical term. Read in any order — the cross-references will get you where you need to go.

findings_observations.intro_paragraph: Based on what we have reviewed, here is where you stand today — what is working, and where we see opportunities to strengthen your position. The strengths are real and rare; we will not understate them. The opportunities are addressed in the recommendation sections that follow.

strengths.body: Profitable, fast-growing business with a defensible niche. HIS posted a three-year revenue CAGR of approximately 23% (2022 $28.4M → 2024 $42.8M) with EBITDA margins expanding from 13.0% to 15.0% in a vertical (data center MEP, healthcare, advanced manufacturing) where demand is structurally accelerating, not cyclical.

Strong operating partner. Derek Chen — vested in his 12% in 2022 — is genuinely capable of running the business, which is what gives a future transaction its optionality. Most owner-led specialty contractors do not have this, and buyer diligence will reward it.

Healthy backlog and recurring revenue mix. $48M of backlog at Q1 2026 (~14 months of forward visibility) with roughly 30% recurring service/maintenance revenue and 70% project — the kind of revenue stack that supports 5.0x – 7.5x EBITDA multiples.

Marcus and Catherine are both insurable at preferred rates. Both excellent health, both non-smoker. This is the precondition for the entire ILIT, buy/sell, and estate-liquidity architecture that follows — many plans we write start with one or both spouses already restricted, and you do not.
Existing 401(k) plan in place at Empower; Marcus deferring at the cap. We are optimizing and adding layers (cash-balance, profit-sharing, mega-backdoor Roth), not starting from scratch.

Engaged ownership willing to invest in long-term planning — which is itself rare and is the single biggest predictor of whether a plan like this gets executed. You came to us before being forced to by a transaction, not after.

bullets: The Kennesaw headquarters / fabrication facility (~$4.2M) is held inside the operating LLC. This exposes the property to operating-business liability and forfeits multiple tax-planning opportunities (cost segregation under OBBBA 100% bonus, §469(c)(7) grouping, separate appreciation tracking, clean transaction structure).

No holding-company layer above HIS. Limits flexibility for a future F-reorganization, charitable gifting of operating-company stock pre-LOI, voting/non-voting recapitalization, and basis planning ahead of a transaction.

Marcus's 88% interest is a single class of voting equity. Without a recap into voting (~10%) and non-voting (~78%) classes, the GRAT, IDGT sale, and SLAT planning all forfeit the 25%–35% lack-of-marketability/minority discount that makes the math work.

Projected federal estate tax exposure of approximately $8M at second death on a projected ~$50M estate (post-OBBBA $30M MFJ exemption × 40%) — and that figure rises sharply with HIS appreciation through the transaction window.

Wills date from 2014, when net worth was approximately $3M. No revocable trust, no ILIT, no SLAT, no GRAT, no IDGT, no QPRT, no dynasty/GST allocation. No DPOA, no healthcare directive, no HIPAA authorization for either spouse — and Olivia (19) needs her own HIPAA at her age.
2014 wills do not name a guardian for Sophie (age 12, born after the wills were drafted). This is the single most acute non-financial gap in the plan.

Beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and life insurance have not been reviewed since the wills were signed. Designations override wills and are the most-overlooked failure point in HNW estate planning.
Georgia PTET election has not been made. At Marcus's K-1 level this leaves approximately $73,000 per year of federal tax savings on the table, with no offsetting cost.

No cost segregation has been performed on the Kennesaw facility. Once the building is moved to a separate LLC, an engineering-based study under OBBBA permanent 100% bonus depreciation produces an estimated $360K – $620K of first-year federal+GA tax savings, paired with a §469(c)(7) grouping election to unlock the depreciation against active K-1 income.
No R&D credit study has been done despite proprietary controls and process-piping engineering work. Estimated annual federal credit of $40K – $120K net of specialty-firm contingent fee.
Catherine is not on payroll. This forecloses her 401(k), individual DI underwriting, backdoor Roth path, and a defensible family-employed-children compensation structure for James and Olivia.

Personal umbrella is $2M against a household net worth in the $33M – $46M range. This is the largest single-event exposure on the personal balance sheet. Auto liability is $250K/$250K, which is below most umbrella underlying requirements.

No buy/sell life insurance funding between Marcus and Derek. The buy/sell mechanics referenced in the operating agreement are unfunded — which means a death event today would force a forced sale, a personal-guarantee draw on the $4M Truist revolver, or a distressed transaction.
Marcus's only DI is a 60% group LTD with an own-occ-then-any-occ definition — inadequate for an HNW owner. No individual DI on Marcus, no DI on Catherine, no business overhead expense policy.

Cyber liability is $1M against a customer mix dominated by hyperscale data-center and healthcare clients with contractual cyber requirements typically $3M+. No key-person life on Derek (separate from the buy/sell layer) or on the controller.

Derek's economic position has not been formalized in years. "Taking care of Derek" is a stated values position, but no SERP, no phantom equity, no documented equity path, no stay-bonus structure exists today.

No Quality of Earnings has been performed; no banker engaged; no fractional CFO supplementing the controller. These are the standard T-12 to T-18 month moves for a business of HIS scale, and the absence of any of them costs real dollars at LOI.

Customer concentration: top 1 = 22%, top 3 = 47%, top 10 = 78%. This is the single largest valuation discount risk in a sale process and is addressable with 18–24 months of deliberate diversification and contract work.

No written succession plan covering owner death/disability — separate from the financial mechanics of buy/sell. Banking signatures, decision authority, customer/vendor communication protocols are undocumented.

Estimated 60%–85% of household net worth is concentrated in HIS equity (range driven by the $32M–$48M valuation band). The taxable brokerage ($1.8M at Schwab, self-directed) and Catherine's Rollover IRA ($340K) are the only meaningful diversification.

Personal cash ($310K Truist) and HIS operating cash are sitting in low-yield bank accounts. A tiered cash structure picks up an estimated $12K – $22K per year on the personal side alone, plus a larger benefit on the business cash position once balance and rate disclosures are in.

Taxable account is in an S&P-500-equivalent fund. No direct indexing, no asset location strategy across taxable / 401(k) / Rollover IRA, no GA-specific muni allocation, no Treasury Direct holdings capturing GA state-tax exemption.
No 529 plans for any of the three children. Front-loading James (16) and Sophie (12) under the 5-year averaging election is one of the highest-leverage moves available on a 0–30 day timeline.

James (16) and Olivia (19) are eligible for legitimate family-business work supporting custodial Roth IRAs — a single year of $7,500 contribution at age 16 compounds to roughly $230K tax-free by age 65.

Eleanor (Marcus's mother, age 78) — the family expects to provide assisted-living or in-home care support within 5–10 years. Her LTC status and balance sheet are not documented; care projections range $300K – $1M+ over the care horizon.

Family communication about wealth has been minimal. Olivia is generally aware; James and Sophie have not been briefed. The trust architecture below holds wealth past age 35 — a deliberate, age-scaled communication and governance plan needs to be built alongside it.

No DAF, no CRUT, no foundation, no formal charitable structure. Marcus's stated intent (trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech) and Catherine's (children's hospital where she serves on the board) are values positions without vehicles. Pre-transaction is the optimal window to fund a DAF with appreciated stock and to evaluate a pre-LOI gift of non-voting Holdco units.
Family foundation is a hard-rule preference post-liquidity (children involved in governance as values-formation). The vehicle should be designed now, funded post-transaction.

recommendations_business.intro_paragraph: The recommendations that follow are organized in execution sequence, not importance order. Sequence matters more than most clients expect — doing the GRAT before the recap, or the cost segregation before the real estate split, gives up most of the benefit. Each section opens with the strategic frame, then names the specific actions, the partner who executes, the dollar exposure, and the timing. Where one section funds or enables another, we have noted it.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: How HIS, the Kennesaw real estate, and Marcus's 88% interest are titled today matters for liability protection, taxation, and the eventual sale or transfer of the enterprise. Four structural changes need to happen in sequence over the next 4–6 months, and the sequence matters: real estate separation first, F-reorganization and holdco second, voting/non-voting recapitalization third, replacement operating agreement fourth. Direct professional fees across all four moves total approximately $48K – $98K — small relative to the seven- and eight-figure planning levers they unlock downstream.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Separate the real estate.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Properties, LLC (Georgia) and transfer the Kennesaw facility from HIS via a §721/§351 contribution-and-distribution structure that preserves basis. Put a written, market-rate triple-net lease in place between HIS (tenant) and Holloway Properties (landlord), with a third-party broker FMV opinion before execution — the IRS will look at this in any audit. Direct cost approximately $15K – $30K. Truist lender consent on the $4M revolver is required and is part of the workstream.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the F-reorganization and stand up Holloway Holdings.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Holdings (Georgia, S-Corp), execute a Plan of Reorganization and Share Exchange so HIS becomes a QSub of the holdco, and file Form 2553 (Holdco S-election) and Form 8869 (QSub election for HIS). Engage specialist M&A counsel — your current general-practice business attorney is adequate for ops but not for this. Direct cost approximately $20K – $35K. The holdco layer is the platform on which the recap, GRAT, IDGT sale, charitable gift of non-voting units, and stock-vs-asset bridge at transaction all sit.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Recapitalize into voting and non-voting interests.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Inside the holdco, recapitalize Marcus's 88% into voting (~10%) and non-voting (~78%) units. The non-voting interest is what gets used in the GRAT, IDGT sale, SLAT, and pre-LOI charitable gift below — it allows you to transfer economic value without giving up control, and it produces a defensible 25%–35% lack-of-marketability/minority discount. Engage a qualified appraiser (a Valuation Provider partner we will introduce) for the discount analysis. Estimated discount value captured at midpoint business value: approximately $7.4M (range $7.7M – $10.7M depending on discount level applied) — that is the dollar amount the downstream estate vehicles get to work with that they otherwise would not. Derek's consent on his 12% being recapitalized is a decision required.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the HIS operating agreement.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current operating agreement predates the holdco, the recap, and a formalized buy/sell. M&A counsel drafts a replacement reflecting the new holdco structure, voting/non-voting classes, transfer restrictions, Derek's 12% economics, and the cross-purchase buy/sell mechanics that REC-RSK-001 in Section 4 funds. Direct cost approximately $5K – $15K; avoided transaction friction at deal-time runs $25K – $100K+.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Calendar an annual operating-agreement audit.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Once the documents are right, they need to stay right. PSA + Business Attorney conduct an annual review of operating, shareholder, buy/sell, and inter-entity agreements ($1K – $3K per year) so that nothing has drifted by the time you go to market.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: If you do the GRAT or IDGT sale before the recapitalization, you give up the 25%–35% valuation discount and lose meaningful estate-tax savings. If you transfer the real estate after the F-reorg rather than before, the contribution-and-distribution mechanics get more complex and the §469(c)(7) grouping in Section 3 becomes harder to clean up. The order is: real estate separation first, F-reorg and holdco second, recap third, replacement operating agreement fourth, then the estate work in Section 4. Done in the right order over 4–6 months, the tax cost of the restructuring itself is essentially zero — and the planning leverage it unlocks runs into eight figures.

the GRAT, IDGT sale, and SLAT planning in Section 4
the buy/sell life funding in Section 5

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Idle business cash earning sub-1% in a 4.5% reserve-yield environment is a self-imposed tax on the operating business. We do not yet have the precise HIS operating cash balance from the controller — that is the unblock we need from the banker — but the structure we install is the same regardless of balance, and the yield pickup scales linearly. The framework is three layers: an operating account at full liquidity, a working reserve in money-market or laddered T-bills, and a strategic reserve in higher-yield treasury or municipal sweep for tax payments and key initiatives.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open a tiered business cash structure at Truist.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Three layers: (1) Operating account — 30 days of payables, full liquidity; (2) Working reserve — 90 days of operating expenses, money market or 1-year laddered T-bills, currently yielding ~4.5%; (3) Strategic reserve — funds earmarked for tax payments, profit sharing, PTET quarterlies, and key initiatives, in a higher-yield treasury or municipal sweep. PSA models the right balance across the three based on the HIS billing cycle and seasonality. Unblock dependency: controller provides current Truist operating balance and 12-month AP/AR cycle; Truist provides current rate sheet on the operating account.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: The yield pickup scales linearly with the idle balance: every $1M moved from a sub-1% operating account to a 4.5% reserve is approximately $35K – $40K per year of pre-tax yield. On a typical $2M – $3M business cash position for a contractor of HIS scale, the annual benefit lands in the range that fully funds the buy/sell life insurance program in Section 5 — before factoring in the federal benefit of PTET in Section 3.

cross_references.display_text: the PTET quarterly funding mechanic in Section 3
the buy/sell life premium in Section 5

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your effective combined federal + Georgia tax rate is approximately 41% — well above what is achievable with PTET, an optimized W-2/K-1 mix, cost segregation paired with §469 grouping, an R&D credit study, and a cash-balance retirement layer. The tax recommendations split into three windows: this year, within 12 months, and long-term considerations leading into the transaction. The order is intentional — earlier moves enable later ones (PTET enables withholding accuracy; the real estate split in Section 1 enables cost segregation; the holdco enables QSBS evaluation).

subsections.heading: 3A. Implement This Year
subsections.intro: Five moves, all on a 0–120 day timeline. Combined estimated annual benefit: approximately $107K – $138K, plus a one-time underpayment penalty avoidance of roughly $40K.

bullets.bold_imperative: File the Georgia PTET election for the current tax year.
bullets.briefing: Estimated annual federal tax savings: approximately $73,000 based on Marcus's projected K-1 income and post-OBBBA SALT cap with phase-out (range $60K – $90K). HIS files the election on Form 600S; CPA models with-vs-without projection and confirms entity cash-flow capacity to fund the PTET liability via quarterly Form 602-ES payments to the Georgia Department of Revenue. The election must be remade each year — calendar it.

bullets.bold_imperative: Re-balance Marcus's W-2/K-1 mix.
bullets.briefing: Engage CPA or compensation specialist (RCReports or similar) for a formal reasonable-compensation analysis benchmarking specialty mechanical contractor CEO comp at $40M+ revenue. Move Marcus's W-2 from $480K toward approximately $360K with the balance flowing as K-1 — net annual benefit $5K – $15K balancing FICA savings against the QBI cap. Update payroll forward-only at Paychex; document the analysis in the HIS tax file for audit defense.

bullets.bold_imperative: Add Catherine to HIS payroll.
bullets.briefing: Document her marketing/admin role with a written job description, time commitment, and deliverables; set defensible salary at $40K – $80K based on industry benchmark; process through Paychex. Net annual benefit $15K – $25K from the combination of business deduction at marginal rate, Catherine's own 401(k) deferral capacity, the path to individual DI underwriting after 6–12 months of W-2 history, a cleared backdoor Roth path once her $340K Rollover IRA is moved, and a defensible family-employed-children structure for James and Olivia.

bullets.bold_imperative: Put James and Olivia on payroll for legitimate work.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $6,300 of annual parents' tax savings ($7,500 per child × 2 eligible children at the household combined ~42% marginal rate) — modest in absolute terms, but the real prize is the custodial Roth IRA capacity it unlocks (covered in Section 11). Document each child's role, set a defensible wage rate, process through Paychex, and maintain quarterly documentation (deliverables, time logs, photos) for audit defense.

bullets.bold_imperative: Implement the Augusta Rule (§280A(g)) for HIS board meetings.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $7,584 of annual federal+GA tax savings on $18,000 of business rent deduction (12 days × $1,500/day) excluded from homeowner income under §280A(g). Obtain 2–3 written quotes from Atlanta event venues to establish the per-day rate; calendar real quarterly board/strategy meetings with agendas, attendees, and minutes; issue invoices from homeowner to HIS. Track day count rigorously — never approach 14 days.

bullets.bold_imperative: Reset withholding and estimated payments now.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $40K of §6654 underpayment penalty avoidable by adjusting against current-year projection (W-2 $480K + K-1 $3.4M). CPA produces a current-year federal and Georgia projection reflecting PTET, cost-seg, cash-balance, and projected K-1; computes required withholding/estimates against the 110% prior-year safe harbor; submit updated W-4 to Paychex and establish quarterly estimated-payment schedule (April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15). Mid-year reconciliation in September.

subsections.heading: 3B. Evaluate Within 12 Months
subsections.intro: Two moves, gated on the real estate separation in Section 1 and the §469(c)(7) grouping election. Combined estimated first-year benefit: approximately $531,594 — the single largest tax line in the plan.

bullets.bold_imperative: Commission an engineering-based cost segregation study on the Kennesaw facility.
bullets.briefing: Estimated first-year federal+GA tax savings: approximately $531,594 (range $360K – $620K) under OBBBA permanent 100% bonus depreciation, paired with §469(c)(7) grouping. Once the building is moved to Holloway Properties, LLC under Section 1, a cost-seg study reclassifies roughly 25%–35% of the $4.2M cost basis into 5- and 15-year property — accelerating depreciation. Engage 2–3 cost-seg firms for feasibility estimates; commission the full study; CPA implements via Form 3115 (catch-up) or Form 4562 (current year). Sequencing: Section 1 must be complete first.
bullets.partner_role: Specialty Tax Credits firm + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: File the §469(c)(7) grouping election in the same return year.
bullets.briefing: This is the move that unlocks the cost-seg accelerated depreciation against Marcus's active K-1 income — without the grouping, the depreciation gets trapped as passive. CPA confirms common ownership and material participation in both Holloway Properties and HIS, files the election with the tax return for the year of grouping, and establishes time-log documentation discipline. The election is binding — material participation must be maintained going forward.

bullets.bold_imperative: Commission an R&D credit feasibility review.
bullets.briefing: Estimated annual federal R&D credit of $40K – $120K net of specialty-firm 25% contingent fee, based on HIS revenue and proprietary engineering work (custom controls, process piping). PSA engages a specialty R&D firm for a no-cost feasibility review; if positive, commission the full study; CPA files Form 6765 incorporating the credit. Renew annually.

subsections.heading: 3C. Long-Term Considerations
subsections.intro: Two moves on a 12–24 month horizon, both transaction-shaped.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate §1202 QSBS via C-Corp conversion or multiplication.
bullets.briefing: Estimated federal capital-gains tax savings: $3.57M – $7.14M (range $0 – $10.71M depending on path) on $15M – $30M of QSBS exclusion at 23.8% LTCG+NIIT, assuming 5+ year hold post-C-Corp conversion. Specialty mechanical contractor is non-SSTB, which is the threshold question. Three paths: Variation A (C-Corp from start — not available retroactively), B (convert mid-stream and hold 5 years — viable only if transaction window is at the longer end of 3–5), C (multiplication via gifting to SLATs and children's trusts to stack per-shareholder $15M exclusions). The decision is firm-policy and client-coupled — surfaced on the Decisions Needed page. Coordinate with Section 4 estate vehicles for the multiplication variant.

bullets.bold_imperative: Build a multi-state PTET / apportionment / nexus map for HIS.
bullets.briefing: HIS operates across GA, TN, AL, the Carolinas, and Northern FL. The personal residence in Georgia keeps Marcus's individual filing simple, but the entity-level PTET, apportionment factors, and any nexus exposure across the operating-states need a coordinated review so the planning above is not unwound by per-state friction. Lower priority than the items in 3A and 3B; CPA-led on a 6–12 month timeline.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Across 3A and 3B alone, the combined annual income-tax benefit lands in the range of approximately $640K – $755K in year one (cost-seg first-year is the dominant line), then settles to roughly $130K – $170K of recurring annual benefit once the cost-seg first-year acceleration runs off. Section 3C is the transaction-window line — $3.57M – $7.14M of capital-gains tax savings if the QSBS path is taken — and it depends on the holdco being in place from Section 1 and on the transaction-timing decision in Section 7.

the SLAT and children's trusts in Section 4 (QSBS multiplication path)
the cash-balance plan in Section 6 (paired tax layer)
