sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your 2014 wills were drafted when net worth was approximately $3M. The household balance sheet today, with HIS at a $32M – $48M valuation, is more than ten times that — and projected federal estate tax exposure at second death is approximately $8M (post-OBBBA $30M MFJ exemption × 40%) and rising. The estate plan does three things in sequence: (1) install the foundation — joint revocable trust, pour-over wills, DPOAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA, Olivia-as-adult documents, and a guardian for Sophie; (2) build the children's trust and dynasty/GST architecture that holds the entire downstream transfer program; (3) execute the wealth-transfer engines — annual exclusion gifts, GRAT, IDGT sale, SLAT, QPRT, and pre-LOI charitable gifts — sized to use lifetime exemption efficiently before the OBBBA $30M MFJ window or HIS valuation moves. Combined cumulative estate tax savings across the layered architecture: low end approximately $11M, midpoint approximately $25M, high-side scenario reaching well into the nine figures via the dynasty/GST allocation.

subsections.heading: 4A. Foundation — 30 to 60 Days
subsections.intro: Nothing else in this section works without these documents in place. Total direct cost approximately $58K – $168K of professional fees, against approximately $50K – $150K of probate cost avoided on the $5M of probate-eligible assets alone.

bullets.bold_imperative: Create a joint revocable living trust and fund it.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts the joint revocable trust for Marcus and Catherine. Fund it by retitling the taxable brokerage, the East Cobb residence, the Blue Ridge home, and the post-recap voting interest into the trust. Probate avoidance on approximately $5M of probate-eligible assets at GA midrange 2% attorney fees: approximately $100K (range $50K – $150K).

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the core estate document set.
bullets.briefing: Pour-over wills, financial DPOAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA authorizations, and guardianship designations for Marcus and Catherine — including a named guardian for Sophie, which the 2014 wills do not have. Separate HIPAA authorization and healthcare/financial POA for Olivia (she is 19 and a legal adult; without these, you cannot make medical decisions or speak to providers about her). Direct drafting cost approximately $8K – $18K. Decision required: guardianship designations for all three children — surfaced on the Decisions Needed page.

bullets.bold_imperative: Complete a beneficiary designation audit across all retirement, insurance, and TOD/POD accounts.
bullets.briefing: Designations override wills and are the single most-overlooked failure point in HNW estate planning. Marcus's 401(k) at Empower, Catherine's Rollover IRA at Schwab, all life insurance policies, and any TOD/POD accounts get reviewed and aligned with the new estate plan. Annual re-audit thereafter.

subsections.heading: 4B. Children's Trust & Dynasty Architecture — 60 to 120 Days
subsections.intro: Everything downstream — annual gifts, GRAT remainder, IDGT distributions, SLAT growth — flows into this architecture. Get this right and the rest works.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish children's trusts with corporate trustee.
bullets.briefing: Cumulative federal estate-tax savings of approximately $4M – $8M (midpoint ~$6.0M) from housing the entire transfer architecture in trusts outside the taxable estate over a 20-year program. Decision required: per-child trusts vs single pot trust; trustee selection (corporate trustee preferred). Honors the family hard-rule: no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35.

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate GST exemption across the dynasty trust(s) at funding.
bullets.briefing: Estimated GST tax avoided: approximately $224M across two generations on $30M of allocated GST exemption growing at 5%/year over 60 years (range $50M – $400M depending on horizon and growth). The number is large because the time horizon is long. Decision required: trust situs — Georgia (RAP-limited) vs. Delaware/SD/NV (perpetual dynasty). Recommended path: site long-duration trusts in Delaware or South Dakota for both perpetuity and the avoidance of GA's 5.19% state income tax on trust income — surfaced on the Decisions Needed page.

bullets.bold_imperative: Site long-duration trusts in Delaware or South Dakota.
bullets.briefing: Qualitatively, siting the dynasty/IDGT/GST trusts in DE or SD avoids GA 5.19% income tax on trust income and supports perpetual duration; over 50-year horizons and meaningful funding the compounded difference reaches mid-seven to low-eight figures. The specific dollar number depends on funding amounts and horizons that are still being structured, so we are not pinning it here — but the decision direction is clear.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up the ILIT and apply for survivorship coverage.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $3.6M of federal estate tax avoided on $9M of ILIT-owned coverage at 40% (range $2.0M – $4.4M depending on face amount). Project estate-tax exposure at first and second death; estate attorney drafts the ILIT with corporate or non-beneficiary individual trustee; CPA obtains EIN; ILIT applies for and owns the policies from inception to avoid §2035 3-year lookback; PSA establishes Crummey notice procedures. Recommended program: $9M ILIT-owned coverage including a $10M survivorship UL on Marcus + Catherine (annual premium estimated $80K – $120K for the survivorship layer, paid via annual Crummey-noticed gifts to the ILIT). Both spouses are insurable at preferred rates today — that window is the asset.

subsections.heading: 4C. Annual Exclusion & Gifting Engine — 0 to 30 Days
subsections.intro: Calendar discipline. Every year you do not gift, you give up that year's transfer capacity forever.

bullets.bold_imperative: Calendar and execute annual exclusion gifts every January.
bullets.briefing: Estimated estate-tax savings of approximately $1.216M on principal alone over 20 years ($19K × 2 spouses × 4 donees × 20 years = $3.04M removed × 40% federal estate tax), and $1.9M – $2.64M including compound growth on gifted assets. Confirm donee list (Olivia, James, Sophie, Eleanor) and gift vehicles (direct cash vs. Crummey trusts to the children's trusts above). CPA files Form 709 to report the gift-splitting election.

subsections.heading: 4D. Wealth-Transfer Engines — 6 to 24 Months
subsections.intro: These run after the holdco, recap, and qualified appraisal are in place. Sequenced so that each one's leverage stacks on the prior.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a 3-Year Zeroed-Out GRAT with $8.2M of non-voting holdco units.
bullets.briefing: Estimated value transferred to the children's trusts free of gift tax: approximately $4.2M (range $0 – $6M depending on business growth and any transaction event during the term). Computed at the current §7520 hurdle of 5.0% (May 2026) and an 18% assumed annual business growth assumption. Any growth above the hurdle passes to the remainder beneficiaries. The strategy is asymmetric in your favor: if the business stalls below the hurdle, the GRAT unwinds at zero gift cost; if it grows or a transaction occurs during/just after the term, the remainder is large.

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute a sale to an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust (IDGT).
bullets.briefing: Estimated additional wealth transferred outside Marcus's estate: $6M – $10M (high case up to $15M if growth + transaction event compound favorably) via a 9-year IDGT installment sale of post-recap non-voting interest at AFR. The grantor pays the trust's income tax — that is a backdoor tax-free gift to the beneficiaries that compounds the leverage. Estate attorney drafts the IDGT with §675(4) substitute power triggering grantor-trust status; seed gift of ~10% of intended sale value; qualified appraisal at sale; promissory note at current AFR.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT) for Catherine's benefit.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $10.8M of federal estate tax avoided over a 25-year horizon by funding a $5M SLAT growing at 7%/year to $27M outside both spouses' estates (range $4M – $18M depending on growth assumption). HEMS distributions to Catherine and the children with an independent trustee; uses a meaningful piece of lifetime exemption now while OBBBA $30M MFJ is in effect. Decision required: funding asset selection.

bullets.bold_imperative: Transfer the Blue Ridge home to a 12-year QPRT.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $840K of federal estate tax avoided (range $0 – $1.5M; $0 if Marcus dies during the 12-year term) by transferring the $1.4M Blue Ridge home to a QPRT at the current §7520 rate of 5.0%, with appreciation passing outside the estate. Decision required: confirm Catherine's real-estate intent and the family's commitment to long-term residence use to qualify QPRT mechanics. Post-term, family pays FMV rent to the trust as additional outside-estate transfer.

bullets.bold_imperative: Make a pre-LOI gift of non-voting Holdco units to a DAF or CRUT.
bullets.briefing: Estimated combined federal income tax + capital gains avoided on a 5% non-voting Holdco gift: $900K – $2.4M (midpoint ~$1.5M) depending on HIS valuation. Must occur before any binding LOI. Engage qualified appraiser; estate attorney prepares formal gift documentation and Form 8283; transfer units to the DAF (per Section 4F) or directly to a $5M FLIP CRUT for a combined deduction and lifetime income stream — the CRUT generates approximately $1.1M of federal charitable deduction at funding (range $1.0M – $1.5M).

bullets.bold_imperative: Pair the CRUT with a wealth-replacement ILIT bailout (Survivorship UL).
bullets.briefing: The CRUT pays charity at the end; without a wealth-replacement layer the family is effectively disinherited by the charitable gift. Indicative scale: at $5M CRUT contribution paired with $5M survivorship UL inside the ILIT, the family receives approximately $5M income-tax-free via the ILIT (range $2M – $8M) and charity receives approximately $5M+ remainder, vs. $2M – $3M to family after estate tax with no charitable component absent the structure. Underwriting and product structure are still being shaped — see the Decisions Needed page for the open inputs.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a NING/DING trust ahead of the transaction.
bullets.briefing: Potential GA 5.19% state-tax avoidance on Marcus's share of the HIS sale capital gain: approximately $1.5M – $2.2M (midpoint ~$1.8M) on $32M – $48M valuation × 88% × near-zero founder basis. Borderline at the GA rate against the $25K – $50K admin cost — viable but not automatic; specialist counsel runs the cost-benefit. Must be funded pre-LOI. Decision required — surfaced on the Decisions Needed page.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a 20-year Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT) as an alternative wealth-transfer engine.
bullets.briefing: Family remainder gift-tax-free at end of 20-year zeroed-out grantor CLAT: $0 if the asset matches the §7520 hurdle (5.00% May 2026) up to roughly $5M+ if the asset earns 8%; very wide range. Highly sensitive to §7520 environment and asset performance — specialist counsel evaluates against GRAT/IDGT alternatives given Holloway charitable intent. Optional layer; not a replacement for the GRAT/IDGT spine.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Done in sequence over the next 24 months, the children's trusts ($4M – $8M), the ILIT and survivorship UL ($2M – $4.4M), the GRAT remainder (~$4.2M), the IDGT sale ($6M – $10M), the SLAT ($4M – $18M), the QPRT (~$840K), the annual exclusion engine ($1.2M – $2.6M), the pre-LOI charitable gift ($900K – $2.4M), and the CRUT/ILIT bailout pairing combine to a layered architecture of approximately $23M – $52M of federal estate-tax savings — before factoring in the dynasty/GST allocation, which is where the long-horizon multi-generational number runs into the nine figures. The order is foundation first (4A), architecture second (4B), gifting engine running continuously (4C), wealth-transfer engines stacked over 6–24 months (4D).

cross_references.display_text: the recap that creates the non-voting interest used by the GRAT, IDGT, SLAT, and pre-LOI gift (Section 1)
the buy/sell life and key-person coverage in Section 5 that interlock with the ILIT
the pre-LOI timing gating for the charitable and NING gifts (Section 7)
the per-child vs pot trust, situs, and guardianship decisions on the Decisions Needed page

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. Our approach is to size protection to specific dollar exposures, not generic rules of thumb. On the business side, four exposures are currently unfunded or under-funded: the buy/sell between Marcus and Derek, owner disability (group LTD only, inadequate for HNW), business overhead during owner disability, and key-person on Derek and the controller. Cyber liability is also under-sized given the hyperscale data-center customer mix. Combined annual premium across the business risk program: approximately $48K – $79K — funded comfortably by the Section 2 cash-yield pickup before any other source.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Fund the Marcus/Derek cross-purchase buy/sell.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Aggregate annual term-life premium $8K – $30K for two cross-purchase policies: Marcus owns approximately $5M face on Derek; Derek owns approximately $5M starting layer on Marcus, age 50–52 preferred non-smoker, 20-year convertible term. Marcus's larger face requires entity-level bonus-and-loan funding documented at AFR for Derek to afford it. M&A counsel drafts the cross-purchase buy/sell agreement with trigger events, valuation methodology, and payment terms — coordinated with the replacement operating agreement in Section 1. The buy/sell mechanics referenced in your current operating agreement are unfunded; this is the single largest unfunded contingent liability between the two owners.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite max-issue individual disability on Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated annual premium approximately $25K (range $15K – $30K) for max-issue individual DI at age 52, own-occ to age 65/67, $25K – $35K/month benefit. Premium paid personally (post-tax) to keep benefits tax-free under §104(a)(3). Audit the existing group LTD's monthly cap, own-occ definition, benefit period, and taxability; document the gap to current and projected household spending; underwrite (medical exam, income documentation, occupation classification); coordinate with the buy/sell disability trigger.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a Business Overhead Expense (BOE) policy.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated annual premium $3K – $8K (midpoint ~$6K) at ~1–2% of covered overhead. Covers HIS rent, payroll, utilities, professional fees, loan payments during owner disability — material given the $4M Truist revolver, 87 FTE, and $48M backlog. HIS as owner and beneficiary; premium deductible as §162 business expense. 24-month benefit period.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add key-person life on Derek and the controller — separate from the buy/sell layer.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Aggregate annual term-life premium approximately $6K (range $3K – $10K) for two key-person policies at ~$2.5M each. Note: Derek is also covered under cross-purchase buy/sell; this layer covers a different economic loss — revenue/transition gap, not equity buyout. §101(j) notice-and-consent must be executed with each key employee BEFORE policy issuance — non-negotiable. CPA files Form 8925 annually for employer-owned life insurance reporting.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Upgrade business cyber liability from $1M to $3M – $5M.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Recommended limit $3M – $5M (midpoint $4M), scaled to the hyperscale data-center customer mix and PII handling for 87 FTE. Hyperscale and healthcare customers typically have contractual cyber requirements at $3M+; current $1M is contractually inadequate. Inventory data exposure (customer PII, employee PII, financial records, IP, and hyperscale customer contractual cyber requirements); quote at $3M – $5M tiers verifying ransomware, social engineering, and business interruption inclusions; coordinate with HIS IT for incident response plan documentation aligned with the new policy.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: The buy/sell funding closes the largest contingent inter-owner exposure on the business; the individual DI and BOE close the largest owner-disability exposures; the key-person and cyber upgrades close the two largest external-event exposures. Combined annual premium of approximately $48K – $79K is materially less than the Section 2 business cash-yield pickup once the tiered cash structure is in place — meaning the entire program is self-funding from existing balance-sheet inefficiency.

cross_references.display_text: the replacement operating agreement and buy/sell terms (Section 1)
the cash-yield pickup that funds the program (Section 2)
the ILIT-owned coverage on the personal-estate side (Section 4)

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: The retirement plan at HIS today is generic — a 401(k) at Empower, no profit-sharing layer, no cash-balance plan, no plan amendment for Roth/after-tax features, no dedicated TPA. Layered properly, the qualified plan and non-qualified retention architecture solve two problems at once: they reduce Marcus and Catherine's current tax bill, and they give Derek and the controller compensation tools that retain them through the transaction. The two purposes interlock — the cash-balance plan must be installed at least 3 years pre-transaction to be durable, and any non-qualified executive comp must be §409A-compliant from day one.

subsections.heading: 6A. Qualified Plan Architecture
subsections.intro: Stacked layers under one §415(c) cap, designed for owner-favorable allocation while clearing nondiscrimination testing.

bullets.bold_imperative: Maximize Marcus's 401(k) deferral now.
bullets.briefing: Estimated current-year federal tax savings of approximately $12,025 on Marcus's $32,500 deferral at assumed 37% marginal rate (range $12K – $25K once Catherine is on payroll under Section 3 and her own deferral is enrolled). Confirm 2026 deferral elections and remaining capacity with Empower; verify Roth catch-up support; submit deferral election. After Catherine is on payroll, enroll her in the 401(k) and set her deferral to the applicable max.

bullets.bold_imperative: Amend the Empower plan document for Roth, after-tax, and SECURE 2.0 features.
bullets.briefing: The plan amendment is a low-cost compliance enabler that unlocks SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up compliance and mega-backdoor Roth capacity. Combined plan-document restatement also incorporates SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0 statutory changes. Standalone economic value is not meaningful — the value flows through dependent recommendations. Coordinate via TPA.

bullets.bold_imperative: Convert Marcus's catch-up to Roth treatment for SECURE 2.0 compliance.
bullets.briefing: Compliance mandate, not an economic-value line. Ensures Marcus's $8,000 catch-up is preserved as Roth treatment and avoids loss of catch-up capacity if the plan does not support Roth. Sequenced after the plan amendment.

bullets.bold_imperative: Layer in cross-tested profit-sharing.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $14,615 of business-level current-year tax savings from a $39,500 profit-sharing layer for Marcus (filling the deferral gap to the $72,000 §415(c) cap) at 37% marginal rate; tax-deferred growth is additional. TPA amends plan document for cross-tested formula and runs annual nondiscrimination analysis across the 87 FTE workforce; CPA funds by entity tax-filing deadline with extensions.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a cash-balance plan layered on the 401(k).
bullets.briefing: Estimated net annual benefit of $50K – $70K (midpoint ~$60K, high case to $110K under optimal cross-tested design; low case zero or negative if the workforce carry-along eats the benefit). Decision required: 87 FTE risk against a 3–5 year minimum durability requirement vs the 3–5 year transaction window — termination of the plan pre-transaction is required (REC-RET-009 below) and reversion-tax planning matters. Engage actuarial firm / DB-specialist TPA for design analysis; workforce projection across 87 FTE; ERISA counsel drafts plan document. Decision surfaced on the Decisions Needed page.

bullets.bold_imperative: Plan the cash-balance plan termination 12–24 months pre-transaction.
bullets.briefing: Pre-transaction termination is required to keep buyer diligence clean; reversion-tax avoidance saves up to 50% of any over-funded excess versus inadvertent reversion. Tied to LOI timing. PSA quarterbacks termination notice, valuation, regulatory filings (Form 5310 if elected, final 5500), distributions, and excess-asset handling.

bullets.bold_imperative: Open mega-backdoor Roth capacity for Marcus.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $25K of annual incremental Roth contribution capacity (range $0 – $39.5K depending on profit-sharing layer size) via after-tax contributions and in-plan Roth conversion. Tax-free growth on the balance compounds for life; immediate tax cost is near zero. Requires the plan amendment above. CPA confirms tax reporting (Form 8606 if applicable, 1099-R review).

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute Marcus's backdoor Roth IRA each year.
bullets.briefing: Estimated $8,600 of annual non-deductible traditional IRA contribution + immediate Roth conversion (50+). Catherine is blocked by the $340K Rollover IRA pro-rata rule until that balance is rolled into a 401(k) — which becomes possible once she is on payroll under Section 3 and the plan accepts the rollover; that path takes us to a $17,200 combined household capacity. CPA files Form 8606 documenting non-deductible basis.

subsections.heading: 6B. Executive Retention & Equity Path
subsections.intro: Treating Derek fairly is a stated values position, not just a planning priority. The retention architecture has three layers: SERP for long-term retention, stay-bonus pool for transaction execution, and a documented equity path for Derek's economic position.

bullets.bold_imperative: Install a §409A-compliant SERP for Derek and the controller.
bullets.briefing: Worked-example COLI premium approximately $25K/year per insured for the primary key employee target ($50K/year benefit at age 65 for 15 years; ~$1M cash value over 20 years). Holloway-style sizing target: $80K/year SERP for Derek and $40K/year for controller (sizing to be confirmed with Marcus). Specialist NQDC counsel drafts §409A-compliant plan; vesting schedule decision required (recommended 7-year cliff vest tied to transaction event or 7 years of continued service, whichever first); §101(j) notice-and-consent on COLI signed BEFORE policy issuance.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate Restricted §162 Bonus as a simpler retention layer.
bullets.briefing: Restricted §162 bonus offers §162 simplicity with retention pull via restrictive endorsement; sizing depends on selected key employee comp targets and vesting schedule. Use case is layered alongside or in place of the SERP for incremental key employees beyond Derek and the controller. Decision required: where it fits relative to the SERP architecture.

bullets.bold_imperative: Create a stay-bonus pool sized to 1–3% of expected transaction value.
bullets.briefing: Stay bonus pool of $400K – $1.2M (midpoint $800K) at 1–3% of $32M – $48M preliminary transaction value. Identify recipients (Derek, controller, lead estimators/PMs); M&A counsel drafts individual stay-bonus agreements with closing trigger, vesting, separation provisions, and non-compete coordination; communicate at banker-recommended timing (typically post-LOI). Payouts at closing + 12-month anniversary administered through Paychex.

bullets.bold_imperative: Formalize Derek's equity path now — separate from buy/sell.
bullets.briefing: Derek's economic position has not been formalized in years. Decision required: chosen mechanism (additional vested units, phantom equity, profits interest path through the holdco, or pre-transaction roll-up of his 12%), target ownership %, and whether the path is consummated pre-transaction or contemplates a post-sale alternative outcome. The dollar impact depends on the chosen mechanism and is not pinned in this section because the structural decision precedes the math.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a profits interest path inside a partnership-taxed layer.
bullets.briefing: Profits interest grants would be tax-efficient under Rev. Proc. 93-27 BUT require restructuring HIS away from S-Corp taxation first — gated on the entity-restructure decision in Section 1 and on grant-percentage selection. Useful only if the household is willing to move HIS to partnership taxation or stand up a separate partnership-taxed grant LLC; not automatic. Three blocked inputs (partnership-taxed entity, candidate recipients, transaction-value-at-grant) need to clear before sizing.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: The plan amendment must come before the mega-backdoor Roth and before the SECURE 2.0 catch-up reset; the cash-balance plan must be installed at least 3 years pre-transaction to be durable, and terminated cleanly pre-LOI; the SERP and stay-bonus pool must be drafted §409A-compliant from day one. Sequencing missteps in this section are not theoretical — they show up at buyer diligence as compliance failures that reduce the price.

cross_references.display_text: the spousal payroll and W-2/K-1 mix in Section 3 that unblocks Catherine's deferral and backdoor Roth
the §101(j) notice-and-consent discipline shared with key-person life in Section 5
the transaction sequencing and stay-bonus communication timing in Section 7
the cash-balance plan go/no-go decision on the Decisions Needed page

sections.label: [OPTIONAL — included because of pre-transaction posture]
sections.intro_paragraph: You have unsolicited inbound interest from both a strategic (regional MEP roll-up) and a financial (lower-middle-market PE) buyer. That interest does not mean you sell — but it does mean the value is real, and that the next 18–24 months are the planning window that determines what the value is when you go to market. The pre-transaction sequence has six workstreams running in parallel and roughly in this order: (1) write the continuity plan, (2) close the customer-concentration discount, (3) install the fractional CFO and management bench, (4) commission the Quality of Earnings, (5) engage the M&A banker, (6) execute the sale process. Combined preserved transaction value across the workstreams: approximately $14M – $30M+ on the $30M baseline.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Write the continuity plan — separate from buy/sell mechanics.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The continuity plan covers operational paralysis on owner death or disability — interim leadership, decision authority, banking signatures, customer/vendor communication. Distinct from the financial mechanics of the buy/sell in Section 5. Decision required: interim leadership designation. Banker confirms succession-ready signature authority on the $4M Truist revolver. Annual review thereafter.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Close the customer-concentration discount — top 1 = 22%, top 3 = 47%.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated $5M – $7M of preserved transaction value (midpoint ~$6M) by closing the 25%–35% concentration discount risk on a $30M baseline through 18–24 months of deliberate diversification and contract work. PSA builds concentration analysis by customer/vertical/geography; Marcus realigns sales-team incentives toward diversification; develops customer expansion plans for non-concentrated accounts; negotiates 3-year-minimum long-term contracts with the 22% hyperscale data-center customer. Quarterly KPI dashboard tracking concentration metrics.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Install a fractional CFO 18 months pre-transaction.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Annual cost approximately $80K – $200K for fractional CFO with industrial-services / specialty contractor M&A experience. Scope: monthly close, board-grade reporting, working capital optimization, transaction prep. ROI is captured through cleaner Q-of-E and banker-process outcomes (next two bullets), not stand-alone. The current controller is capable but operationally focused — the gap is M&A-readiness, not basic accounting.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Close management bench gaps to remove the owner-dependence discount.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated $6M – $12M of preserved transaction value (midpoint ~$9M) by closing the 20%–40% owner-dependence discount on a $30M baseline. Org chart analysis identifying critical function gaps (COO/operations, CFO/finance, sales leader, lead technical). Decision required for each gap: hire externally, promote internally, or fractional contract. Compensation packages coordinated with stay bonus, SERP, and any phantom equity in Section 6. Quarterly milestone tracking aligned with transaction timing.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Commission Quality of Earnings 12–18 months pre-transaction.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated preserved transaction value $1.5M – $4.5M (midpoint $3M) — 5%–15% of $30M baseline preserved through cleaner buyer diligence, net of $75K – $125K Q-of-E engagement cost. Engage independent Q-of-E firm separate from the existing CPA for independence. Provide financial records, customer concentration data, and revenue detail; receive report 6–10 weeks after engagement; address issues identified (clean books, normalize accounting, document customer concentration). Refresh Q-of-E 6 months later if material time elapses before going to market.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Engage M&A counsel and a competitive sell-side banker.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Estimated net premium captured from a competitive sale process: $4.4M – $9.3M (midpoint $6.8M) — 15%–30% premium on the $30M baseline less estimated 1%–2% banker fee on the post-premium transaction. Banker selection: shortlist 2–3 boutique LMM M&A finalists with industrial-services / specialty contractor specialty. M&A counsel negotiates engagement letter (success-fee structure, minimums, expense reimbursement, carve-outs). CIM development with banker; client review and approval; run sell-side process — buyer outreach, first-round indications, second-round LOIs, diligence and closing.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: The preserved-transaction-value lines stack: customer concentration ($5M – $7M) + management bench ($6M – $12M) + Q-of-E ($1.5M – $4.5M) + competitive banker process ($4.4M – $9.3M) sum to approximately $17M – $33M of preserved or captured value on a $30M baseline. None of this happens at closing — it happens 12–24 months before, which is exactly the window you are in now. A buyer will pay for what you can demonstrate; everything in this section is the demonstration.

cross_references.display_text: the entity work in Section 1 that the banker process depends on
the pre-LOI gating for charitable gifts and NING in Section 4
the stay-bonus, SERP, and cash-balance termination timing in Section 6

recommendations_personal.intro_paragraph: The business is the first place we invest. Personal portfolios are the second — they are what give you flexibility, independence, and a soft landing if anything happens to the business. With roughly 90% of household net worth tied up in HIS today, the personal-side work in this section is about building the structures that protect what you have already built and prepare the family for what comes next: cash that earns a real yield, insurance sized to actual exposure, a portfolio designed for the transaction window rather than against it, an education and family-employment program for the children, and the foundational estate documents that should have been refreshed years ago. None of this is glamorous. All of it is overdue.

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: You have approximately $310,000 of personal cash earning effectively nothing at Truist and $1.8M of self-directed taxable brokerage at Schwab that has not been managed for tax efficiency. Restructured into the right layers and the right vehicles, the same balances produce roughly $18,000 of additional annual yield on the cash side and $13,500–$22,950 of after-tax alpha on the portfolio side — without taking on additional risk. With a 90%+ concentration ratio in HIS today, the priority for the personal portfolio through the transaction window is deliberate diversification, tax efficiency, and discipline. It is not the place to add more concentration or chase private markets.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Layer the personal cash into operating, emergency, and near-term tiers.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: On the $310K personal cash position, build a three-tier structure: 1–2 months of lifestyle in the Truist operating account, 6–12 months of emergency reserve in a high-yield savings or brokerage money market at ~4.5%, and a 12–36 month near-term layer in a Treasury ladder at ~4.7%. Estimated incremental yield: approximately $18,000 per year over the current near-zero placement. We will run this with you quarterly.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Use Treasury Direct or Schwab Treasuries for the near-term layer.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Treasury interest is exempt from Georgia's 5.19% state tax — a small but real edge over money-market funds. On the personal $120K near-term layer the GA-tax savings is roughly $290/year; combined with the business reserve laddering that figure rises into the low thousands. We will set up auction auto-reinvestment at maturity and coordinate with the CPA at filing to ensure the Form 500 state-exemption schedule is applied.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the S&P-equivalent fund with a direct indexing SMA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: On the $1.8M Schwab taxable brokerage, move from a single index fund into a separately managed account that replicates the index using the underlying ~250 stocks and systematically harvests losses on individual positions. Typical after-tax alpha is 50–100 bps annually — approximately $9,000–$18,000 per year on the current balance, midpoint roughly $13,500. Particularly valuable in years with heavy K-1 income, which describes every year through the transaction window.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Optimize asset location across taxable, 401(k), and Catherine's IRA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Across the combined ~$3.06M of taxable brokerage and retirement accounts, place tax-inefficient assets (REITs, taxable bonds, high-turnover funds) inside the tax-deferred wrappers and tax-efficient assets (broad equity index, munis, qualified-dividend payers) inside the taxable brokerage. Estimated after-tax improvement: approximately $22,950 per year at 75 bps on the combined balance. Constrained by the Empower 401(k) menu — we will work within what is available and address menu gaps through REC-RET-007 plan amendment.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a Georgia-specific muni SMA inside the taxable account.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: For the fixed-income sleeve of the taxable brokerage (we'd target ~40% of the account, roughly $720K), use a Georgia-specific municipal bond SMA. Interest is exempt from federal tax and from Georgia state tax — a meaningful pickup at your marginal rate. Estimated after-tax yield differential vs. taxable corporates: approximately $4,300 per year at the current sleeve size, scaling to $12,000+ as the sleeve grows post-transaction. Minimum credit quality AA-/A1; avoid AMT private-activity bonds.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Run quarterly tax-loss harvesting with cross-account wash-sale awareness.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Once direct indexing is in place this is largely automated, but we will still run a quarterly review across all taxable accounts looking for harvestable losses, and a year-end pass to bank carryforwards against future realized gains (transaction proceeds, GRAT remainder distributions). The cross-account piece matters: Catherine's $340K Rollover IRA is in scope for wash-sale matching even though it is a separate account. Annual harvested-loss summary delivered to the CPA.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Hold the line against pre-transaction concentration drift.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Resist the pre-IPO pattern of moving the personal portfolio aggressively into private markets, single-stock concentration, or other construction-sector exposures before a sale. Your concentration risk is already 60–85% of household net worth through HIS itself — the personal portfolio should be the diversification, not another concentration. We will track concentration ratios quarterly and run any opportunity that comes through the door against this rule.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: The cash and investment changes above produce approximately $18,000/year of incremental cash yield, $13,500/year of direct-indexing tax alpha, $22,950/year of asset-location alpha, and $4,300/year of muni after-tax pickup — together roughly $58,000–$60,000 per year of after-tax improvement on assets you already own. None of this requires additional capital, additional risk, or a sale event. It is fee-and-friction the household has been paying every year by default.

cross_references.display_text: the parallel business cash-management work in Section 2
the Roth and 529 vehicles funded by this strategy in Section 11
the personal estate framework these accounts will be retitled into in Section 12

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. Your current personal coverage was built when the household balance sheet was a fraction of its current size — $2M of personal umbrella against a household net worth that runs $33–46M depending on how HIS is valued, $250K/$250K auto liability that does not even meet a serious umbrella's underlying requirements, and a single group LTD policy on Marcus that caps replacement income at a fraction of actual earned income. Three coverage repairs in the next 30–120 days close the largest exposures. A fourth — long-term care — is a deliberate decision Marcus and Catherine will make together once underwriting offers are in hand.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Raise the personal umbrella to $35M – $50M.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current $2M umbrella is materially under-sized against a $33–46M net worth — a single auto accident with serious injury could reach into the operating-business equity. We will quote umbrella coverage at $25M, $35M, and $50M tiers, using specialty carriers (Chubb, AIG, Pure) for layers above $10M which are not available through Allstate. Recommended target: $35M, with annual review post-transaction when net worth increases sharply.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Raise auto and homeowner liability to umbrella underlying minimums first.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Before binding the new umbrella, raise primary auto liability to at least $500K/$500K (currently $250K/$250K) and homeowner liability to $1M. Carriers will not honor an umbrella claim if the underlying limits do not meet their requirements, which is a known landmine on the existing Allstate stack. The premium increase is modest — typically a few hundred dollars per year — and this is a gating step for everything else in this section.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite max-issue individual disability coverage on Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The HIS group LTD provides 60% replacement, 24-month own-occ then any-occ — meaningfully inadequate against current $480K W-2 plus $3.4M K-1. Underwrite Marcus for max-issue individual DI ($25K–$35K/month benefit, own-occ to age 65/67, typically through MassMutual's max-issue program). Estimated premium approximately $25,000/year, paid personally with after-tax dollars to keep the benefit tax-free under §104(a)(3). Coordinates with the buy/sell disability trigger in Section 4.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite individual DI on Catherine after 6–12 months of W-2 history.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Once Catherine is on the HIS payroll (Section 3), build a 6–12 month W-2 record so individual DI becomes underwritable. She becomes more difficult to insure with age and with any health change; the window to lock in a preferred-rate own-occ policy is now while both spouses are in excellent health.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate hybrid LTC coverage on both spouses.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: At ages 52 and 49, both in excellent health, this is the right window to lock in long-term care capacity at favorable rates. Hybrid life+LTC product structure means the family receives a death benefit if LTC is never used — addressing the most common objection to standalone LTC. For a $100K–$250K single-pay (or 10-pay equivalent) per insured, expect roughly $200K–$750K of death benefit and $400K–$1.5M of LTC benefit pool depending on carrier and structure. We will get underwriting offers from at least two carriers; the budget and pay-structure decision is yours to make once the offers are in hand.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a personal D&O endorsement for Catherine's nonprofit board service.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Verify entity D&O adequacy at both nonprofit boards Catherine serves on, and add a personal D&O endorsement (typically $1K–$3K/year through an HNW carrier) to backstop entity coverage gaps. Board-related claims against directors personally are uncommon but not rare, and entity D&O at small nonprofits is often thin. This is small premium for meaningful sleep-at-night value.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: Together these moves bring personal coverage into proportion with actual exposure: a ~$35M umbrella sized to net worth, primary limits raised to support it, individual DI on both spouses sized to actual earned income rather than a group cap, hybrid LTC underwritten while both of you are insurable at preferred rates, and a personal D&O backstop for Catherine's board work. Total incremental annual premium across these layers is approximately $35K–$60K — small relative to what is being protected, and significantly smaller than the buy/sell program in Section 4 that protects the business side of the same balance sheet.

cross_references.display_text: the buy/sell, BOE, and key-person program in Section 5
the ILIT and survivorship UL coordinated through estate planning in Section 12

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: The personal-side tax work picks up where the business-side work leaves off. With Marcus's W-2 and K-1 producing AGI in the $3.6M range, three things matter most: getting Catherine onto the payroll so the household captures retirement, insurance, and family-employment leverage that is currently unavailable; calibrating withholding and estimates so you do not pay roughly $40,000 of underpayment penalty in a year of cost-seg-driven income volatility; and using a clean §280A(g) home-rental program to move ~$18,000 of business deduction onto the return without rental-income offset. Each of these is small in isolation; together they are roughly $30K–$40K per year of structural improvement plus a one-time ~$40K penalty avoidance.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add Catherine to the HIS payroll at a defensible salary.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Catherine performs real marketing and family-management work for HIS but is currently $0 W-2. Document her role with a written job description, time commitment, and deliverables; set defensible salary at $40K–$80K based on industry benchmarks for a marketing/admin coordinator role. Estimated net annual benefit approximately $15,000–$25,000 across the deduction at marginal rate, 401(k) deferral capacity, individual DI insurability after 6–12 months of W-2 history, and the clearing path for Catherine's $340K Rollover IRA pro-rata block (Section 11). Maintain quarterly documentation file with deliverables and time logs.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Recalibrate withholding and estimates against the current-year projection.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current withholding/estimate pace will not match a year that includes PTET payments, cost-seg-driven depreciation, cash-balance contributions, and projected K-1. The CPA will produce a current-year federal and Georgia projection and compute required payments against the 110%-of-prior-year safe harbor (AGI > $150K). Estimated penalty avoidance: approximately $40,000 (computed at ~$1M underpayment × 0.5 × 8% federal underpayment rate). Mid-year reconciliation in September.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Implement the §280A(g) Augusta-rule home-rental program.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Rent the East Cobb residence to HIS for legitimate quarterly board/strategy meetings — capped at 12 days per year, never approaching 14. Obtain 2–3 written quotes from comparable Atlanta-area event venues to establish a defensible per-day rate (~$1,500); calendar quarterly meetings with real agendas, attendees, and minutes; issue invoices from homeowner to HIS; CPA records the deduction on the HIS return and confirms the homeowner is NOT reporting rental income. Estimated annual federal+GA tax savings approximately $7,584 on $18,000 of business rent at the combined ~42% marginal rate. Documentation discipline is everything on this one — the IRS audits Augusta-rule cases when the documentation is thin.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Coordinate per-state apportionment across HIS's 5+ operating states.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: HIS operates in 5+ states; each has its own PTET rules, apportionment formulas, and nexus thresholds. Low-priority module given Georgia residence, but worth the CPA's annual review to avoid double-counting income across states and to capture state-specific PTET optimizations as more states add elective regimes. Net dollar impact is small at current footprint; meaningful if HIS expands footprint pre-transaction.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Catherine on payroll produces approximately $15K–$25K/year of net household benefit; the Augusta-rule program adds another ~$7,600/year; recalibrated withholding avoids roughly $40,000 of underpayment penalty in the current year alone. These are not the largest line items in the plan — but they are the cleanest, the fastest to implement, and they unlock downstream moves (Catherine's DI insurability, her 401(k) deferral capacity, the path to clear her Rollover IRA pro-rata block) that have value well beyond the line-item tax savings.

cross_references.display_text: the business-side tax program in Section 3 (PTET, cost segregation, R&D, QSBS)
the retirement and Roth implications of Catherine's payroll in Section 11

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: The personal-side retirement and family work has two simultaneous goals. First, fully use the qualified-plan capacity available to both of you — deferral, profit-sharing fill to the §415(c) cap, mega-backdoor Roth, backdoor Roth IRA — across roughly $65,000 of incremental Roth and pre-tax capacity per year that is currently unused. Second, set up the next-generation infrastructure: 529s front-loaded for James and Sophie, custodial Roths for the working children, the family communication and governance norms that the 2014 will did not contemplate, and a discovery conversation with Eleanor before any care need arises. The hard rule from your goals — no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35 — is honored throughout.

subsections.heading: 11A. Maximize Retirement Capacity
subsections.intro: Marcus is at the deferral limit but the household is leaving roughly $65,000/year of additional tax-advantaged capacity on the table. Closing those gaps requires one plan amendment, a small payroll change for Catherine, and disciplined annual execution.

bullets.bold_imperative: Amend the Empower 401(k) to support Roth deferrals and after-tax contributions.
bullets.briefing: The current plan is generic — no Roth catch-up support and no after-tax / in-plan-conversion rails. Amend through Empower (and, if needed, a dedicated TPA) to enable Roth deferrals, Roth catch-up (SECURE 2.0 compliance for high earners), after-tax contributions, and in-plan Roth conversions. The amendment is low-cost and unlocks the mega-backdoor Roth and Roth-catch-up moves below.

bullets.bold_imperative: Maximize Marcus's $32,500 deferral with Roth catch-up where required.
bullets.briefing: Confirm Marcus's 2026 deferral elections target the full $32,500 (regular + catch-up). Estimated current-year federal tax savings approximately $12,025 at 37% marginal. Once Catherine is on payroll, enroll her in the 401(k) and target her deferral cap as well — household deferral capacity rises to ~$65,000 combined.

bullets.bold_imperative: Use the mega-backdoor Roth to fill Marcus's §415(c) gap.
bullets.briefing: Once the plan amendment is in place, Marcus contributes after-tax dollars up to whatever §415(c) space remains after deferrals + match + cross-tested profit-sharing layer (Section 6), then triggers periodic in-plan Roth conversion. Estimated annual Roth capacity: approximately $25,000 mid-case (range $0–$39,500 depending on profit-sharing layer size). Tax-free growth on this balance compounds for life; immediate tax cost is near zero.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run the backdoor Roth IRA for Marcus annually.
bullets.briefing: $8,600/year non-deductible traditional IRA contribution converted to Roth the next business day. Marcus has no contaminating pre-tax IRA balance, so the pro-rata rule does not bite for him. Catherine is blocked by her $340K Rollover IRA — once she's on payroll, evaluate rolling that balance into the HIS 401(k) to clear the pro-rata block and double household backdoor-Roth capacity to $17,200/year. CPA files Form 8606 documenting non-deductible basis annually.

subsections.heading: 11B. Education & Family-Employed Children
subsections.intro: Three children, three different planning horizons. Olivia (19, sophomore at UGA) is in the spend-down phase; James (16) has 6+ years to college and is eligible for legitimate family-business work; Sophie (12) has 13+ years for compounding to do its work. The right vehicles look different for each.

bullets.bold_imperative: Front-load Georgia Path2College 529s for James and Sophie.
bullets.briefing: Open a Georgia Path2College 529 per beneficiary with parent owners. Year-1 deployment: $190,000 each into James's and Sophie's 529s, gift-split between Marcus and Catherine using the 5-year averaging election on Form 709. Olivia receives an appropriate annual contribution rather than a full front-load given her short remaining horizon. At a 7% compound return over 13 years on the front-loaded principal, James's and Sophie's accounts project to roughly $700K–$900K combined at full deployment.

bullets.bold_imperative: Calendar the 15-year 529-to-Roth rollover capacity per beneficiary.
bullets.briefing: SECURE 2.0 allows lifetime $35,000 per beneficiary of unused 529 funds to roll into the beneficiary's own Roth IRA — provided the 529 has been open 15+ years and the beneficiary has earned income. Total household capacity: approximately $105,000 of next-generation Roth seed capital. Rolled over years 15–20 and compounded tax-free for 35–45 years, this projects to roughly $800K–$2.5M of next-gen Roth value depending on returns. We track the 15-year clock per account.

bullets.bold_imperative: Put James and Olivia on HIS payroll and fund custodial Roths.
bullets.briefing: Document legitimate roles for James (16) and Olivia (19) — admin, marketing support, file/data work — at defensible wage rates compliant with Georgia and federal child-labor minimum wage rules. Estimated annual parents' tax savings approximately $6,300 at $7,500/child × 2 eligible × ~42% combined marginal. More importantly, fund a custodial Roth IRA at Schwab for each up to earned income (max $7,500). A single $7,500 contribution at age 16, compounded at 7% for ~49 years, projects to approximately $230K of tax-free retirement value — per year of contribution. Documentation discipline (W-2s, time logs, photos of work product) is non-negotiable for audit defense.

bullets.bold_imperative: Open the discovery conversation with Eleanor.
bullets.briefing: Marcus's mother Eleanor (age 78, Marietta, widowed, no LTC believed in place) is likely to need meaningful financial support within 5–10 years. Industry-standard projections put 3–7 year care needs in Georgia at $300K–$1M+. We will host a values-and-finances conversation with Eleanor and Marcus to inventory her assets, income, and any existing LTC coverage; project care-cost scenarios; evaluate whether LTC underwriting is still feasible at her age and health; and update her own healthcare directive, DPOA, and HIPAA documents alongside yours. Annual review thereafter.

subsections.heading: 11C. Family Governance & Communication
subsections.intro: Catherine has been explicit that family communication about wealth has been minimal and that she wants this to change deliberately. That is the right instinct, and it matters more than most families realize: trustees ultimately exercise discretion based on what they know about the family's values, and children inherit best when they understand the values that produced the wealth — not just the dollar figure.

bullets.bold_imperative: Codify a Holloway family mission statement.
bullets.briefing: Capture the values that already animate your decisions — dignity of trades work (Marcus), community health access (Catherine), stewardship over consumption — into a brief written family mission statement. This is not branding; it is the language trustees will use to exercise discretion when you are not in the room and the document the children will read at age 35 when they receive distributions. PSA facilitates; estate attorney coordinates so the mission statement integrates into trust drafting and (eventually) the family-foundation governing documents.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish an annual family meeting cadence.
bullets.briefing: Begin annual family meetings with age-appropriate participation: Olivia in full from year one, James starting next year, Sophie when she's 14–15. Cadence is annual; agenda is values + structure + decisions, not balance sheets. This builds the shared understanding the family will need by year five when foundation governance gets real.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run an age-scaled wealth-readiness curriculum for the three children.
bullets.briefing: Olivia (19) gets immediate one-on-one work — she is generally aware of family wealth and is the closest to inheriting in any scenario. James (16) gets an intermediate program: budgeting, investing basics, business fundamentals, the meaning of K-1 vs W-2. Sophie (12) gets an introductory program oriented around values and stewardship. Each is preparing for material trust distributions deferred to age 35 per your hard rule.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: The retirement moves alone produce roughly $50,000–$65,000 per year of incremental tax-advantaged capacity (deferral, mega-backdoor Roth, backdoor Roth IRA) — which over the 3–5 year transaction window adds $200K–$300K of additional Roth and pre-tax balances at near-zero current tax cost. The family-employed-children path layers another ~$6,300/year of parents' tax savings on top of meaningful next-gen Roth seed capital. None of the family-governance work shows up as a dollar line item, but it is the work that makes the trust distributions in Section 12 actually function the way you want them to.

cross_references.display_text: Catherine's payroll setup in Section 3
the cash-balance, profit-sharing, and SERP architecture in Section 6
the children's-trusts and dynasty/GST structure in Section 12

sections.numbered_heading: 12. Personal Estate Planning, Charitable Strategy & Trust Architecture
sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your wills date from 2014, when household net worth was approximately $3M. There is no revocable trust, no ILIT, no DPOA, no healthcare directive, no HIPAA authorization, no beneficiary review since the last child was born, and no guardian named for Sophie. This is the single largest unaddressed area of the plan — and at a projected estate-tax exposure of $11M+ growing toward $17M+ as HIS appreciates, it is also the single largest unfunded liability on your balance sheet. The estate work breaks naturally into three layers: foundation (the documents that should already exist), wealth-transfer architecture (the trusts that move value out of your estate efficiently), and post-liquidity charitable structure (the family-foundation vehicle Catherine has indicated as a hard preference).

subsections.heading: 12A. Foundation — Refresh the Document Set
subsections.intro: These are the moves that should happen in the first 30–60 days. Without them, every downstream trust is sitting on a cracked slab.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish a joint revocable living trust and retitle assets.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts a joint revocable living trust for Marcus and Catherine. Fund the trust by retitling investment accounts, the East Cobb residence, the Blue Ridge vacation home, and the post-recap Holdco voting interest into the trust. Probate avoidance on roughly $5M of probate-eligible assets at Georgia's typical 1–3% attorney fee saves approximately $50K–$150K, with a midpoint around $100K — separate from the privacy and continuity benefits.

bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the 2014 wills, POAs, and healthcare directives.
bullets.briefing: Pour-over wills, financial POAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA authorizations, and explicit guardianship designations for James and Sophie. Olivia, age 19, also needs her own HIPAA authorization and healthcare/financial POA — without these, you have no legal authority over her medical care if something happens. Direct drafting cost approximately $8,000–$18,000 for the full document set; this also avoids potential conservatorship costs of $5K–$25K and ongoing probate friction.

bullets.bold_imperative: Audit and update every beneficiary designation.
bullets.briefing: Beneficiary designations override wills — they are the single most-overlooked failure point in HNW estate planning. Inventory and update designations on Marcus's 401(k), Catherine's Rollover IRA, all life insurance policies (current term and forthcoming ILIT-owned), and any TOD/POD accounts so they align with the new trust architecture. PSA owns this list and re-audits annually.

bullets.bold_imperative: Begin the annual exclusion gifting program in January.
bullets.briefing: $19,000 × 2 spouses × 4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, Eleanor) = $152,000 of annual exclusion capacity each year. Over 20 years that removes $3.04M from the estate (principal only); at 40% federal estate tax that is approximately $1.216M of estate-tax savings — and that excludes compound growth on the gifted assets, which pushes the savings range to $1.9M–$2.6M at 7% growth. Start January of the implementation year; calendar annually; CPA files Form 709 to elect gift-splitting.

subsections.heading: 12B. Wealth-Transfer Architecture
subsections.intro: Once the recap (Section 1) gives us a non-voting class of Holdco units to work with, the wealth-transfer engine has four interlocking pieces. Each one removes value from your estate; together they remove $30M+ over the planning horizon at near-zero gift-tax cost.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish children's trusts to receive the transfer architecture's output.
bullets.briefing: Children's trusts are the receiving vessels for annual exclusion gifts, GRAT remainders, IDGT distributions, and SLAT growth. Decision pending: per-child trusts (more administrative, more flexibility, separation of beneficiary economics) versus a single pot trust (simpler, more parental). Either way: corporate trustee preferred, distribution standards aligned with your hard rule of no unrestricted access before age 35. Cumulative federal estate-tax savings from housing the entire transfer architecture in these trusts: approximately $4M–$8M over a 20-year program.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a 3-Year Zeroed-Out GRAT with $8.2M of non-voting Holdco units.
bullets.briefing: After the recap and a qualified appraisal of the non-voting interest, fund a 3-year zeroed-out GRAT. Annuity payments are calibrated by the CPA to drive the gift value to zero at the current §7520 hurdle rate (5.00%, May 2026). Any growth above the hurdle passes to the children's trusts free of gift tax. Estimated remainder transferred: approximately $4.2M at 18% assumed business growth; $0 if business stalls below the §7520 hurdle (in which case the GRAT unwinds at zero gift cost, hence "zeroed-out"); potentially $6M+ if a transaction event lands during or just after the term.

bullets.bold_imperative: Sell additional non-voting Holdco interest to an IDGT for a 9-year note.
bullets.briefing: Draft an Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust with a §675(4) substitute power. Make a seed gift of approximately 10% of intended sale value, then sell additional non-voting Holdco units to the IDGT in exchange for a 9-year promissory note at AFR. "Defective" is a term of art — it works exactly as intended: the trust is a separate entity for estate-tax purposes (assets pass to beneficiaries outside your estate) but you continue to pay the income tax on its earnings, which is a backdoor tax-free gift to the trust beneficiaries. Estimated additional wealth transferred outside Marcus's estate: approximately $6M–$10M, potentially $15M if growth and a transaction event compound favorably during the note term.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish a SLAT for one spouse's benefit funded with $5M of appreciating assets.
bullets.briefing: A Spousal Lifetime Access Trust drafted with HEMS distributions to the beneficiary spouse plus children, independent trustee, funded with appreciating assets (likely additional non-voting Holdco units or post-transaction securities). Estimated federal estate tax avoided over a 25-year horizon at 7% growth: approximately $10.8M, range $4M–$18M depending on growth rate. The SLAT is also the natural place to sit any §1202 multiplication strategy if HIS converts to C-corp (Section 3).

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate $30M of GST exemption to dynasty trust(s) at funding events.
bullets.briefing: Across the children's trusts, IDGT, SLAT, and ILIT, allocate GST exemption explicitly at every funding event so all four structures grow as GST-exempt dynasty vehicles. Decision pending: trust situs — Georgia (limited by RAP) versus Delaware/SD/NV (perpetual dynasty + state-tax avoidance). Estimated GST tax avoided across two generations on $30M growing at 5% over 60 years: approximately $224M (range $50M–$400M depending on horizon and growth). This is a generational decision — the magnitude is real because the time horizon is.

bullets.bold_imperative: Transfer the Blue Ridge vacation home into a 12-year QPRT.
bullets.briefing: Blue Ridge ($1.4M, no mortgage) is a clean QPRT candidate if Catherine and Marcus are committed to long-term family use. Transfer to a Qualified Personal Residence Trust with a 12-year retained term; appreciation passes outside the estate; you continue to use the home rent-free during the term and pay FMV rent to the children's trusts thereafter. Estimated federal estate tax avoided: approximately $840,000, range $0–$1.5M (zero if Marcus dies during the 12-year term; higher with stronger appreciation). Decision pending: confirm long-term use intent before drafting.

bullets.bold_imperative: Form an ILIT and acquire $9M–$10M of survivorship UL.
bullets.briefing: The Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust is drafted with a corporate or non-beneficiary individual trustee and applies for the policies from inception (avoiding §2035's 3-year lookback). Underwrite a $10M survivorship universal-life policy on Marcus and Catherine, both currently insurable at preferred rates. Annual premium approximately $80K–$120K, funded via Crummey-noticed gifts to the ILIT. Federal estate tax avoided on the death benefit: approximately $3.6M at the 40% rate on the recommended $9M program (range $2M–$4.4M). The death benefit lands at the second death — exactly when the projected $8M+ federal estate-tax bill comes due.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a NING/DING trust before any binding sale agreement.
bullets.briefing: A Nevada or Delaware Incomplete-Gift Non-Grantor trust formed and funded before Marcus signs a binding LOI on HIS could move his share of the sale capital gain outside Georgia's 5.19% income tax. On the projected Marcus 88% × HIS valuation × ~zero basis × 5.19% calculus, savings range approximately $1.46M–$2.19M, midpoint ~$1.8M, against admin cost of $25K–$50K. Georgia's rate is on the borderline of NING/DING economic threshold, so specialist counsel evaluates viability before we proceed — but if it pencils, the savings dwarf the friction.

subsections.heading: 12C. Charitable Architecture
subsections.intro: Catherine's preference for a family foundation post-liquidity is on the record as a hard rule, not just a DAF. The path there is sequential: a DAF in the near term to capture pre-transaction deduction value, a CRUT before any binding sale agreement to convert appreciated Holdco units into a deduction + lifetime income stream, the family foundation post-liquidity, and an optional CLAT layer if §7520 conditions and family circumstances warrant.

bullets.bold_imperative: Open and fund a Donor-Advised Fund this year.
bullets.briefing: Open a DAF at Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, or a community foundation; fund with $50K–$100K of appreciated Schwab brokerage securities (NOT cash) to capture both the income-tax deduction at FMV and the avoided capital gain on the appreciation. Appoint Marcus and Catherine as primary advisors and the children as successor advisors — this is also part of the family-governance curriculum in Section 11. Estimated annual federal+GA tax savings approximately $28,000 at the $75K midpoint contribution (range $21K–$42K).

bullets.bold_imperative: Pre-LOI, gift non-voting Holdco units to the DAF or a CRUT.
bullets.briefing: Before any binding sale agreement, gift a 5% non-voting Holdco interest to either the DAF (simpler) or a Charitable Remainder Unitrust (lifetime income to Marcus + Catherine, charitable deduction at funding, charity remainder). On a $32M–$48M HIS valuation the combined federal income-tax + capital-gains savings is approximately $900K–$2.4M. This must be done before — not after — Marcus signs an LOI; once a binding sale agreement exists, the assignment-of-income doctrine kills the strategy. Coordinate with the M&A counsel and banker so the DAF/CRUT appears as shareholder of record before the transaction process closes.
bullets.partner_role: PSA / Estate Attorney / Valuation Provider / M&A Counsel / CPA

bullets.bold_imperative: If using the CRUT path, draft a FLIP-CRUT with a wealth-replacement ILIT pairing.
bullets.briefing: A FLIP-CRUT (5% unitrust, 20+ year horizon) on $5M of appreciated Holdco units produces a federal charitable deduction at funding of approximately $1.1M (range $1.0M–$1.5M) plus a lifetime income stream and a charity remainder. The ILIT-owned survivorship UL above is the wealth-replacement leg — the family is not disinherited by the charitable gift because the ILIT death benefit replaces what went to charity. At the indicative $5M asset / $5M survivorship UL scale, family receives ~$5M income-tax-free via ILIT and charity receives ~$5M+ remainder, versus ~$2–3M to family after estate tax with no charitable component absent the structure.

bullets.bold_imperative: Form the Holloway Family Foundation post-liquidity.
bullets.briefing: After the transaction closes, form a private family foundation as the long-term governance vehicle Catherine has identified as the hard-rule preference. Funding amount and annual giving target are decisions for the post-transaction conversation, not now — but the architecture (Marcus's trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech, Catherine's children's-hospital priorities, eventual children involvement in governance) is on the record. The DAF balance accumulated through the transaction window grants into the foundation as the transition vehicle.

bullets.bold_imperative: Consider a 20-year Zeroed-Out CLAT if §7520 stays in current range.
bullets.briefing: A 20-year zeroed-out grantor CLAT funded with $5M produces a charitable annuity stream for 20 years and a family remainder gift-tax-free at term-end. Highly sensitive to §7520 (currently 5.00%) and asset performance: at 5% asset return, family remainder is approximately $0; at 8% return, ~$5M; at 10% return, ~$10M+. We will evaluate against the GRAT/IDGT alternatives once the rate and asset environment are clearer; this is a 12–24 month decision, not a now-decision.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Stacked together — annual exclusion gifting (~$1.2M–$2.6M), children's-trust architecture (~$4M–$8M), GRAT remainder (~$4.2M), IDGT sale (~$6M–$10M), SLAT growth (~$10.8M), QPRT on Blue Ridge (~$840K), ILIT death benefit (~$3.6M), GST allocation across all of the above (~$224M two-generation) — this program is designed to remove the great majority of HIS's appreciated value from your taxable estate and your children's taxable estates. Some of these numbers are point estimates; some are 30–60 year projections that require a wide range. All of them are achievable only if the foundation in Section 12A is set first, the Section 1 recap creates the non-voting interest these vehicles need to be funded with, and the sequencing in the Implementation Roadmap is followed in order. The 2014 wills were appropriate for a $3M household. The architecture above is what fits a $40M household that is approaching a transaction. The gap between the two is what this section is built to close.

cross_references.display_text: the recap and non-voting class created in Section 1
the §1202 QSBS multiplication strategy that pairs with the SLAT in Section 3
the survivorship UL underwriting in Section 9
the per-child vs. pot trust decision and the GA vs. DE/SD situs decision in Decisions Needed
the 30 / 60 / 120-day sequencing in the Implementation Roadmap

meeting_cadence_intro.intro_paragraph: A plan that is delivered and never revisited goes stale within 12 months. Here is how we will work together going forward. The cadence below mixes structured touchpoints (quarterly tax, semi-annual investment review, annual plan review) with triggered reviews when something material changes — an inbound buyer, a §7520 rate move, a child entering a new life stage, a tax-law change, an Eleanor health event. PSA quarterbacks; you set the pace.
immediate_next_steps: Review this document. Mark anything you want to discuss, push back on, or have us re-frame. Take your time — there is no "right answer" pace.
Hold the delivery meeting (already scheduled) to walk the document together and confirm direction.
Decide on the top 3 priorities from the Executive Summary list. We will execute against those first while the rest of the plan moves in parallel.
Resolve the open items on the Decisions Needed page — guardianship for the children, per-child vs pot trust structure, trust situs, cash-balance plan go/no-go, and the QSBS path.
Schedule the first implementation check-in for 30 days from delivery; we will have introductory calls with the estate attorney, M&A counsel, and CPA candidates lined up by then.

$5M limit. Other business coverages: GL $2M occurrence/$4M aggregate; workers comp in place; commercial auto fleet in place; cyber $1M (likely under-insured given customer mix). No buy/sell life funding; no key person coverage on Derek or controller.

goals_priorities.intro_paragraph: These are the goals we identified together during discovery. Every recommendation in this plan ties back to one or more of them. We will revisit and refine these at every annual review.

goals.what_this_means_in_practice: Ranked goals: (1) Maximize after-tax sale proceeds — position HIS for a 3-5 year transaction window with structural and tax planning that compounds value retained; (2) Take care of Derek — treat his 12% fairly through any transition, both economically and as key continuity person; (3) Estate planning — reduce projected $11M+ estate-tax exposure through layered trusts, gifts, and life insurance; (4) Entity & real estate structure — separate Kennesaw real estate from operating company, build holding-company layer for future flexibility; (5) Tax strategy — capture federal benefit of GA PTET, optimize W-2 vs K-1 mix, evaluate cost segregation, §469 grouping, cash-balance plan; (6) Children's provision without demotivation — fund education and meaningful head start with controls preventing unrestricted access before age 35; (7) Catherine's real estate vision — build structural capacity for her to deploy meaningful capital into real estate development post-exit; (8) Risk management — match life, disability, and key-person coverage to actual exposures; (9) Personal investment diversification — build wealth outside the business, systematic taxable brokerage funding, asset location; (10) Philanthropic vehicles — DAF near-term, family foundation post-liquidity with children eventually involved in governance.

Marcus: trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech (his alma mater); values dignity of skilled trades work. Catherine: children's hospital where she serves on the board (community health access); sees philanthropy as how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. Near-term: DAF. Post-liquidity: family foundation with children involved in governance (hard-rule preference, not just a DAF).

Children provided for but with restrictions — no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35 (hard rule). Catherine wants children involved in foundation governance as values-formation mechanism. Family communication about wealth has been minimal: Olivia generally aware, James and Sophie not yet briefed; Catherine wants this to change deliberately as the children enter adulthood, with cautious timing. Anticipated future support of Marcus's mother Eleanor (assisted-living/in-home care within 5-10 years).

Treat Derek (12% owner) fairly through any transition — both as economic owner and as key continuity person; will not sell to a buyer who would relocate operations or eliminate Derek's role; intact business is non-negotiable (no strip-and-flip buyers). Derek's economic position has not been formalized in years and needs clarity. Marcus open to 1-3 years post-sale transition role. Employee communication is closed; only Marcus, Catherine, Derek, and PSA know.

Marcus values the dignity of skilled trades work — built HIS deliberately as a place where field people are paid and respected like the professionals they are. Wants to extend that through trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech. Catherine values community health access (her board work at the children's hospital) and sees philanthropy as how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. Both deeply value the operating partner relationship with Derek; "taking care of Derek" is a values position, not just a planning priority.

glossary.intro_paragraph: Plain-English definitions for the technical terms used in this plan.

entries.plain_english_definition: Allows up to 100% federal tax exclusion on the sale of qualified small business stock (QSBS) held more than 5 years, subject to limits. Generally requires C-Corp structure.
Generally disallows passive losses (like rental real estate) from offsetting active business income — but a grouping election can change that.
Disability insurance that reimburses the business for fixed operating costs while the owner is disabled.
Life insurance owned by the business on key employees, often used to informally fund deferred compensation arrangements.
A charitable account where you take the deduction at funding but recommend grants to charities over time. The simplest charitable vehicle.
Replaces a portion of income (typically 60–70%) if you cannot work due to illness or injury.
A trust that pays you back the full value of what you contribute (plus an IRS-set interest rate) over a set term. Any growth above that rate passes to your heirs free of gift tax.

A trust structured so the grantor pays the income tax on the trust's earnings (an additional gift-tax-free transfer) but the assets are outside the grantor's estate. "Defective" is a term of art — it works exactly as intended.
A trust that owns a life insurance policy so that proceeds pass outside the insured's taxable estate.
Non-binding outline of deal terms, typically signed before due diligence in a transaction.
An election that pays state tax at the entity level rather than the individual level — bypassing the $10,000 SALT cap on personal returns.
A detailed analysis of a business's earnings done by an independent firm, typically commissioned ahead of a transaction.
A non-qualified retirement plan funded by the employer for select key employees, typically with vesting schedules to encourage retention.

body_paragraphs: Will Bearden is a registered representative of and offers securities and investment advisory services through MML Investors Services, LLC. Member SIPC. Supervisory Office: 6 Concourse Pkwy NE, Atlanta, GA 30328 United States.
PSA Wealth is not a subsidiary or affiliate of MML Investors Services, LLC or its affiliated companies.

This document contains tax-related considerations. Nothing in this document should be construed as tax or legal advice. Always consult with your own tax or legal advisor concerning your specific situation.

Estimates and projections shown in this plan reflect assumptions about future tax law, valuations, growth rates, and your circumstances. Actual results may vary materially. Specifically: (i) business valuation ranges are preliminary and not a formal valuation opinion; (ii) tax savings estimates are based on current law as of the plan date and your projected income; (iii) GRAT and IDGT projected wealth transfers depend on actual business performance versus the IRS-set hurdle rate during the trust term, and adverse performance can reduce or eliminate the planning benefit; (iv) insurance premium estimates are based on standard underwriting and may differ following actual underwriting.
This plan is a living document and will be updated based on the cadence outlined in Meeting Cadence & Next Steps.
